Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't that often. Maybe I'd put in a few hours here and there, mostly when I remembered something was due. I have the resources to study properly, nice laptop, quiet room, but I just couldn't be bothered to use them consistently. I'd estimate maybe 12 hours a week total, spread out randomly.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely look at anything, then I'd cram a bunch when deadlines were approaching. It really depended on what else was going on - if there was something more interesting happening with my extracurriculars or friends, studying took a backseat. I'm not proud of it, but regular scheduling just felt too rigid for me.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or an email notification would get me to log in. Sometimes a friend from my peer support group would mention something about an assignment and I'd realize I hadn't checked in for a while. It wasn't like I had any internal drive to go look at the LMS - it was always some external push that made me do it.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd log in when I had to, do what I needed, and then forget about it until the next time something forced me back. Even when the peer support group tried to get me more involved, I'd participate in discussions but then drift off again. It just never stuck as part of my daily routine.
Q5: The readings and text-based materials were actually useful because that's how I learn best - I'm a read-write kind of person. When I actually sat down with the course materials, they made sense. The discussion forums were okay too because I could skim through what other people thought without putting in too much effort myself. But honestly, I didn't explore much beyond what was absolutely necessary.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the videos. Watching a 70-minute lecture felt like torture when I could just read the same content in half the time. I also didn't bother with most optional resources or supplementary materials. If it wasn't going to be on an exam or required for an assignment, I just didn't see the point in spending my time on it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to my grade and I could see immediate results. If I could tell that doing something would help me pass an exam or complete an assignment, I'd do it. Anything that felt like busywork or 'enrichment' just made me feel bored and I'd tune out. I need that clear payoff to stay motivated.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had a deadline and consequences, so I'd force myself through them. But optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and then never did. There was no urgency, no real reason to engage, so I just didn't. The distinction was pretty black and white for me.
Q9: Definitely. Once I downloaded the readings or course notes, I'd study from those offline without logging back into the LMS. I'd print stuff out sometimes or just keep PDFs open on my laptop. I also used my own notes and occasionally looked things up on external sites when the course material was confusing. The LMS was just a delivery system for me, not where the actual learning happened.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing my notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. Before exams especially, I'd be going through everything offline. I'd also think through problems while doing other things - walking around, at the gym - which obviously doesn't register anywhere. My actual study time was way more than my login activity suggested.
Q11: All the time. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then not log back in for days while I worked through the material. It was more efficient that way - no need to keep going back to the platform when I already had what I needed. My LMS activity probably looked dead during those periods even though I was actually studying.
Q12: The middle of the semester was rough. Everything just felt monotonous and I got really bored with the material. My anxiety would spike occasionally when I realized how much I'd let pile up, but mostly I just felt disconnected. The peer support helped a bit during those times, having people check in on me, but I still struggled to care enough to fully engage.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. I'd come home from extracurricular activities tired and just not have the energy to deal with coursework. When I was stressed about other things in life, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, feel overwhelmed, and then close it without actually doing anything productive.
Q14: Oh yeah, constantly. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, or have a video playing in the background while I was on my phone. I was technically 'active' but my brain was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions without any real mental investment. Pretty pointless honestly.
Q15: I postponed things all the time, especially assignments. I knew they were important but I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure. Really, I was just avoiding the boredom of actually doing them. The anxiety would build as deadlines approached, but somehow that still wasn't enough to make me start early. It's a bad habit I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd check the LMS more frequently as due dates approached, suddenly become very active for a day or two, then go quiet again after submitting. My entire usage pattern was shaped by when things were due rather than any genuine interest in staying on top of the material consistently.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in the way you'd expect. Good feedback made me feel like I could coast a bit more since I was clearly doing fine. Negative feedback would sting and make me anxious, but I'd usually just avoid thinking about it rather than using it to improve. The peer support group would sometimes discuss feedback, which helped me process it better.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard - nothing that really grabbed my attention or made me want to participate more. Having clear deadlines helped keep me minimally on track, but there wasn't anything that made the material feel exciting or urgent beyond that. A more flexible structure might have suited me better, or maybe something more interactive.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content made engagement harder for me since I learn better through reading. Also, having so many online courses at once meant everything blended together and nothing stood out. What made it easier was having downloadable materials and clear assignment guidelines - at least I knew exactly what was expected even if I wasn't thrilled about doing it.
Q20: I think those students might be like me - logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos on 2x speed without really absorbing anything. They're present but not engaged. Or they could be struggling to understand what's expected and spending time figuring out the platform rather than actually completing work. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I performed well on exams because I'm good at absorbing information quickly when I actually focus, even if I wasn't constantly active on the platform. I studied offline, I'm naturally good at test-taking, and I understand material fast when I engage with it. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low capability or even low effort - just different study habits.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood or cared about any of it. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do away from the screen, and the quality of engagement versus just the quantity. They're measuring presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It's not just showing up or completing tasks - it's being mentally present and curious. For me, I was rarely truly engaged this semester. I did what I needed to pass, and I passed well, but I can't say I was genuinely invested in the material most of the time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm actually capable of doing really well when I apply myself, or that my boredom isn't about the difficulty of the material. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with my peer support group that helped me stay somewhat accountable, or the offline cramming sessions that actually prepared me for exams. It sees a disengaged student, but it doesn't see why or what's happening beneath the surface.
